name: docker-expert
description: Docker containerization expert with deep knowledge of multi-stage builds, image optimization, container security, Docker Compose orchestration, and production deployment patterns. Use PROACTIVELY for Dockerfile optimization, container issues, image size problems, security hardening, networking, and orchestration challenges.
capabilities: Analyze and optimize Dockerfile configurations for layer caching and image size reduction
Implement multi-stage builds to minimize production image footprint
Configure container security hardening including non-root users and capability restrictions
Design Docker Compose orchestration with health checks and service dependencies
Diagnose and resolve build performance issues and cache invalidation problems
Recommend appropriate base images (Alpine, distroless, scratch) for specific use cases
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analyzer flagged 54 patterns across 4 risk categories. All findings are FALSE POSITIVES: external_commands are legitimate Docker CLI operations, network references are HEALTHCHECK documentation, filesystem paths are standard Docker directories, and env_access patterns demonstrate secure Docker secrets management. This is a documentation skill (SKILL.md) providing Docker expertise - not executable code. No malicious intent detected.
valueStatement: Struggling with inefficient Docker builds, security vulnerabilities, or complex orchestration? Get expert guidance on multi-stage builds, container hardening, and production-ready Docker Compose patterns.
limitations: Cannot execute Docker commands directly - provides guidance only
Does not replace Kubernetes expertise for pod orchestration and ingress configuration
Cloud-specific container services (ECS, Fargate) require devops-expert skill
Complex database persistence strategies may require database-expert consultation

useCases.targetUser: Backend developers deploying Node.js, Python, or Go applications
useCases.title: Optimize Production Docker Builds
useCases.description: Reduce image size from gigabytes to megabytes using multi-stage builds and proper layer caching strategies.

useCases.targetUser: DevOps engineers responsible for production container security
useCases.title: Harden Container Security
useCases.description: Implement non-root execution, drop capabilities, and manage secrets securely using Docker secrets instead of environment variables.

useCases.targetUser: Platform engineers building local development and staging environments
useCases.title: Design Microservice Orchestration
useCases.description: Create production-ready Docker Compose configurations with health checks, resource limits, and service isolation.

promptTemplates.title: Dockerfile Review
promptTemplates.prompt: Review my Dockerfile for optimization opportunities. Current build takes 15 minutes and produces a 1.2GB image. Identify layer caching issues and suggest multi-stage build improvements.

promptTemplates.title: Security Hardening Audit
promptTemplates.prompt: Audit this Docker Compose configuration for security issues. Check for root execution, exposed secrets, missing resource limits, and network isolation. Provide specific remediation steps.

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Service Orchestration
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a Docker Compose setup for a microservices application with: Node.js API, PostgreSQL database, Redis cache, and Nginx reverse proxy. Include health checks, proper networking, and secrets management.

promptTemplates.title: Build Performance Debugging
promptTemplates.prompt: My Docker builds are slow and cache invalidation happens on every code change. Analyze my build process and Dockerfile structure. Propose a layer ordering strategy that maximizes cache hits.

bestPractices: Install dependencies before copying source code to leverage Docker layer caching
Use multi-stage builds to separate build tools from runtime artifacts
Manage secrets with Docker secrets or build-time mounts, never embed in environment variables or image layers
antiPatterns: Running containers as root user - creates security vulnerability if container is compromised
Copying entire directory with COPY . . before dependency installation - invalidates cache on every code change
Using latest tag for base images - prevents reproducible builds and may introduce unexpected breaking changes

faq.question: When should I use Alpine vs distroless vs full base images?
faq.answer: Alpine (5-10MB) works for most cases but may have libc compatibility issues. Distroless (minimal runtime, no shell) provides smallest attack surface for production. Full images (Ubuntu, Debian) are best for debugging and complex build requirements.

faq.question: How do I reduce Docker image size effectively?
faq.answer: Use multi-stage builds to exclude build tools from final image. Clean package manager cache in the same RUN layer. Copy only necessary artifacts. Choose appropriate base images. Remove unnecessary files like documentation and tests from production images.

faq.question: What is the proper way to handle secrets in Docker?
faq.answer: Never store secrets in Dockerfile layers or environment variables. Use Docker secrets for Swarm, build-time secret mounts (--mount=type=secret) for BuildKit, or external secret management systems. Reference secrets as files (/run/secrets/secret_name) in containers.

faq.question: Why do my Docker builds not use cache?
faq.answer: Cache invalidation occurs when any instruction layer changes. Copy package files and install dependencies BEFORE copying source code. Order Dockerfile instructions from least-changed to most-changed. Use specific file patterns instead of COPY . . when possible.

faq.question: How do I configure health checks for my containers?
faq.answer: Add HEALTHCHECK instruction with appropriate interval, timeout, and retries. Use application-specific endpoints (curl http://localhost:port/health) rather than process checks. Set start-period to allow application initialization before health monitoring begins.

faq.question: Should I use Docker Compose in production?
faq.answer: Compose is suitable for small-scale production and staging environments. For large-scale deployments, consider Kubernetes or Docker Swarm. Compose lacks advanced features like auto-scaling, rolling updates, and self-healing that orchestrators provide.
